title: EdgeRelations
lean_code_with_comments:
def adjacent (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop := G.sus v1 v2

def edgeInto (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop := G.hus v1 v2

def edgeOutOf (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop := G.tuh v1 v2
lean_code_without_comments:
def adjacent (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop := G.sus v1 v2

def edgeInto (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop := G.hus v1 v2

def edgeOutOf (G : CDMG Node) (v1 v2 : Node) : Prop := G.tuh v1 v2
lean_explanation:
This row formalises the three numbered items of `def_3_3` — adjacency, edge-into-a-vertex, and edge-out-of-a-vertex — as two-argument predicates on a CDMG. The definitions sit inside `namespace CDMG`, so each attaches to a `CDMG Node` value `G` via dot notation: writing `G.adjacent v1 v2` parses left-to-right like the LN's $v_1 \sus v_2 \in G$, and similarly for `G.edgeInto v1 v2` and `G.edgeOutOf v1 v2`. The binder `Node : Type*` is the ambient vertex carrier — matching the silent LN assumption that vertices live in some fixed type — and `[DecidableEq Node]` is inherited from the surrounding `variable` line because the underlying `Finset` operations need to decide pair equality in the kernel.

Each definition is a one-line wrapper around one of the seven primitives introduced in `def_3_2` (the `CDMGNotation` block). `adjacent` unfolds to `G.sus v1 v2`, which itself is the three-disjunct `G.tuh v1 v2 ∨ G.hut v1 v2 ∨ G.huh v1 v2` — "some edge of any type lies between $v_1$ and $v_2$". `edgeInto` unfolds to `G.hus v1 v2 = G.hut v1 v2 ∨ G.huh v1 v2` — "the edge between $v_1$ and $v_2$ has an arrowhead pointing at the focal vertex $v_1$". `edgeOutOf` unfolds to `G.tuh v1 v2 = (v_1, v_2) \in G.E` — "there is a directed edge from $v_1$ to $v_2$".

The two-argument shape `_ G v1 v2 : Prop` is the same for all three, but their symmetry behaviour differs. `adjacent` is symmetric in its two arguments (because `sus` is — the bidirected disjunct uses the `hL_symm` field bundled on the structure). `edgeInto` and `edgeOutOf` are *not* symmetric: the first argument is the **focal vertex** — the one with the arrowhead for `edgeInto`, the source of the directed edge for `edgeOutOf` — and the second is "the other endpoint". No symmetry or coincidence lemmas are stated here, because `def_3_3` itself does not assert any; later rows derive what they need on demand.

The one Lean-side idiom worth flagging for a mathematician new to the encoding is that these are `Prop`-valued definitions, not `Bool`-valued. Lean separates `Prop` (the logical universe, where proofs are first-class terms like `h : G.adjacent v1 v2`) from `Bool` (a data type that would force `decide` invocations at use sites). The `Prop` choice makes the predicates compose naturally with the rest of the chapter-3 logical machinery.
design_choices:
**`def`, not `abbrev`, for all three relations.** An `abbrev` would unfold eagerly during elaboration and `simp`, so any tactic touching `G.adjacent v1 v2` would immediately rip it open into the three-disjunct `sus` and then into `Finset` memberships. Using plain `def` keeps the named relation opaque by default; downstream proofs reason about `adjacent`, `edgeInto`, `edgeOutOf` as primitive concepts and only invoke `simp only [CDMG.adjacent]` (etc.) when a step genuinely needs a single controlled layer of unfolding. This matches the convention already in use throughout `CDMGNotation`.

**`edgeOutOf` is a single `tuh`, *not* the disjunction `tuh ∨ hut`.** This is the load-bearing design call for the row. Item 3 of the LN lists two writings — $v_1 \tuh v_2$ and $v_2 \hut v_1$ — but an explicit operator addition identifies these as the *same edge*: both unfold to $(v_1, v_2) \in E$. A naive `G.tuh v1 v2 ∨ G.hut v1 v2` would be wrong, because `G.hut v1 v2 = G.tuh v2 v1` is the *opposite* directed edge (from $v_2$ to $v_1$); including it would mean a directed edge $v_2 \to v_1$ would also count as "out of $v_1$", contradicting the LN's intent. In a CDMG only directed edges (members of $E$) have tails — bidirected edges have arrowheads at both ends — so "out of $v_1$" is precisely "directed edge with source $v_1$".

**`edgeInto` uses `hus` (focal-on-left), not `suh` (focal-on-right) or a fresh disjunction.** Item 2 of the LN splits into two sub-clauses with the focal vertex on opposite sides of the writing ("into $v_1$" vs "into $v_2$"). Under the same writing-identification addition these are one notion parameterised by the focal vertex, so they collapse to a single predicate. The `hus` spelling is picked because the LN's primary "into $v_1$" sub-clause is already written with the focal vertex on the left, and `CDMGNotation.hus` already provides the matching disjunction `G.hut v1 v2 ∨ G.huh v1 v2` — no duplication, no new code.

**Two-argument predicates of the focal vertex, not edge-as-object.** A cleaner-looking alternative would treat an edge `e : Node × Node` as the primary object and ask whether `e` has a head or tail at a focal vertex `v`. We reject this for chapter 3 because every downstream consumer (`def_3_4` walks, `def_3_5` parent/child/sibling sets, `claim_3_1` no-arrowhead-into-$J$) pattern-matches on the LN macros $\hus / \suh / \tuh / \hut$ directly, not on an edge-as-object abstraction. Introducing the abstraction here would force every consumer to convert back and forth, and would raise an extra design question (is `e` intended to live in $E$, in $L$, or in their union?) with no LN-side answer.

**Thin renames of existing notation, not independent disjunctions.** Each of the three definitions is literally a one-line wrapper around a `CDMGNotation` primitive (`sus`, `hus`, `tuh`). Re-spelling the underlying `Finset` memberships here was rejected: any future refactor — for instance, adding a fourth edge type — would force updates in two places instead of one. The thin-wrapper approach also makes adjacency definitionally equal to `sus`, so `simp only [CDMG.adjacent]` reaches the underlying $\mathrm{Finset}$ disjunction in a single step.
